channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: A sugar key to the origins of life has been discovered in interstellar space - CNN
item.description:
1. A sugar key to the origins of life has been discovered in interstellar space  CNN
  2. A Sweet Surprise: Scientists Find Sugar Deep in Our Galaxy  The New York Times
  3. Space jam: astronomers detect ‘raspberry sugar’ on dust cloud in Milky Way  The Guardian
  4. Detection of a four-carbon sugar in interstellar space  Nature
  5. In a sweet discovery, astronomers find sugar lurking in the space between stars  NBC News

item.title: T rex fossil ‘Gus’ sells for $50.1m at New York auction, setting new record - The Guardian
item.description:
1. T rex fossil ‘Gus’ sells for $50.1m at New York auction, setting new record  The Guardian
  2. A T. rex found in South Dakota could smash auction records — and possibly disappear forever  CNN
  3. Gus the T-Rex Sells for Record $50.1 Million USD at Sotheby's  Hypebeast
  4. T. rex sells for $50 million, becoming the most expensive dinosaur fossil ever auctioned  CNBC
  5. Tyrannosaurus Rex most expensive dinosaur ever sold  BBC

item.title: Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman - The New York Times
item.description:
1. Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman  The New York Times
  2. Solution to Feynman’s reverse sprinkler puzzle also applies to “silly sprinklers”  Ars Technica
  3. 'Silly sprinklers' put in reverse to further unravel decades-old physics puzzle  Phys.org
  4. NYU Researchers Identify Physical Mechanisms Behind Reverse Sprinkler Motion  geneonline.com
  5. Feynman’s Famous Physics Problem of a Sprinkler Running in Reverse has a Surprising New Solution  The Debrief

item.title: Mathematics formula found on Maya wall rivals insights of ancient masters - Nature
item.description:
1. Mathematics formula found on Maya wall rivals insights of ancient masters  Nature

  4. Hidden in a Guatemalan Ruin For 1,200 Years Was The Signature of a Maya Mathematical Genius  ScienceAlert

item.title: Scientists Finally Solved the Mystery of Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ It’s Bad News for Our Future - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Scientists Finally Solved the Mystery of Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ It’s Bad News for Our Future  Gizmodo
  2. Around 252 million years ago, volcanoes across what is now Siberia erupted repeatedly for more than a million years, releasing perhaps 100,000 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide and helping wipe out roughly 90 per cent of marine species in the deadliest mas  Space Daily
  3. Researchers confirm cause of Earth’s biggest mass extinction  Stanford Doerr School of Sustainability
  4. Scientists finally solved the mystery of Earth's greatest mass extinction  ScienceDaily
  5. Metabolic vulnerability confirmed as cause of Earth’s biggest mass extinction  Open Access Government

item.description:
1. Frame-dragging observations validate Einstein yet again  Phys.org

  4. Armenian scientist co-authors Nature study confirming Einstein’s theory with record precision  Public Radio of Armenia
  5. Scientists just confirmed Einstein’s greatest theory—again  Scientific American

  3. Dark Matter and the Hidden Fifth Dimension  Scientific Frontline
  4. Dark matter's secret address revealed? Scientists say it may be hiding in fifth dimension; here's what the  The Economic Times

item.title: When Voyager 1 launched in 1977, each of its main computer systems had less than 70 kilobytes of memory — smaller than a typical phone photo — and that 1970s hardware is still carrying out commands from Earth nearly five decades later - Space Daily
item.description:
1. When Voyager 1 launched in 1977, each of its main computer systems had less than 70 kilobytes of memory — smaller than a typical phone photo — and that 1970s hardware is still carrying out commands from Earth nearly five decades later  Space Daily
  2. Voyager's signal now reaches Earth as a whisper measured in fractions of a billionth of a watt, fainter than almost anything our antennas were built to hear  Space Daily
  3. Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day  ScienceAlert
  4. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 with onboard computers holding less memory than a single photo on a modern phone — and that 1970s machine is still running, sending data back to Earth from interstellar space  ScienceBlog.com
  5. One Light-Day Away: Voyager's Epic Voyage  Rediff

item.title: Quantum-gravitational mechanism could explain the universe's homogeneity - Phys.org
item.description: Quantum-gravitational mechanism could explain the universe's homogeneity  Phys.org

item.title: Six massive landslides discovered on icy Pluto - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Six massive landslides discovered on icy Pluto  Phys.org
  2. Pluto has landslides  Science News
  3. 6 massive landslides discovered on Pluto, one of them twice the size of Manhattan | Earth's landslides are driven heavily by gravity | Inshorts  Inshorts
  4. Traces of massive landslides detected on Pluto  Universe Space Tech
  5. Pluto has six confirmed landslides now, and their unusually long runouts are giving scientists a clue about how icy worlds move  The Times of India

item.title: Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year.  Space Daily
  2. In 2023, seismologists at the Australian National University, reading the echoes of earthquakes ringing back and forth through the whole planet, confirmed Earth hides an innermost core inside its inner core, a distinct iron ball roughly 1,300 kilometres across w  Space Daily
  3. Academia Sinica - Research Findings | Earth’s mantle locally heats up our planet’s core  sinica.edu.tw
  4. Earth’s inner core is an iron-rich ball as hot as the surface of the Sun, kept solid only because the crushing pressure of the entire planet raises the temperature at which iron can melt  Space Daily

item.title: Chinese Spacecraft Approaches Mysterious Object Near Earth - Yahoo News New Zealand
item.description:
1. Chinese Spacecraft Approaches Mysterious Object Near Earth  Yahoo News New Zealand
  2. China releases 1st photo of Earth's elusive 'quasi-moon' Kamo'oalewa  Space
  3. In Deep Space, China Meets Earth’s ‘Amazing’ Quasi-Moon  The New York Times
  4. China’s Tianwen-2 Space Probe Has Rendezvoused With Earth’s Quasi-Moon  WIRED
  5. A new picture of Earth’s ‘mini moon’  Coeur d'Alene Press

item.title: Why is Venus hotter than Mercury, when Mercury is closer to the Sun? - The Conversation
item.description:
1. Why is Venus hotter than Mercury, when Mercury is closer to the Sun?  The Conversation
  2. Amid the Stars: Earth’s Evil Twin  mountain-news.com
  3. About 50 kilometres above Venus’ surface, the crushing heat and pressure below give way to temperatures and air pressure surprisingly close to Earth’s, a region NASA has described as the most Earth-surface-like environment in the solar system — at least b  Space Daily
  4. What would happen to a human being on the surface of Venus?  Universe Space Tech

item.title: New Horizons flew past Pluto so quickly that an encounter nine years in the making was effectively over within hours — but the data gathered during that brief passage took another 15 months to trickle home - Space Daily
item.description:
1. New Horizons flew past Pluto so quickly that an encounter nine years in the making was effectively over within hours — but the data gathered during that brief passage took another 15 months to trickle home  Space Daily
  2. On This Day, July 14: NASA's New Horizons gets closest look yet at Pluto  Yahoo
  3. On This Day: NASA's New Horizons gets closest look yet at Pluto  upi.com
  4. NASA’s New Horizons travelled 3 billion miles over 9 years to reach Pluto – but it was moving too fast to stop when it arrived  The Times of India
  5. When the New Horizons spacecraft flew past Pluto in July of 2015 after a nine-and-a-half-year journey from Earth, it was traveling so fast — roughly 32,000 miles per hour — that it had less than 30 minutes to photograph the entire dwarf planet, and the images  Space Daily

item.title: Antarctica is classified as a desert because it receives less precipitation than the Sahara, yet it holds about 70 per cent of the planet's fresh water, locked in an ice sheet averaging over two kilometres thick across the continent - Space Daily
item.description: Antarctica is classified as a desert because it receives less precipitation than the Sahara, yet it holds about 70 per cent of the planet's fresh water, locked in an ice sheet averaging over two kilometres thick across the continent  Space Daily

item.title: Einstein’s relativity allows time travel into the future, and we have already measured it: atomic clocks flown around Earth on aeroplanes came home showing a different time from clocks left on the ground - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Einstein’s relativity allows time travel into the future, and we have already measured it: atomic clocks flown around Earth on aeroplanes came home showing a different time from clocks left on the ground  Space Daily
  2. Physics says time travel into the future is not a thought experiment — every astronaut who has orbited Earth has returned a fraction of a second younger than they would have been if they had stayed on the ground  Space Daily
  3. The wild physics that could actually be used to build a time machine  New Scientist
  4. Scientists discovered time travel may already be happening  MSN

item.title: Astronomers Found a Hidden Swarm of Space Junk Threatening Earth's Most Valuable Orbit - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Astronomers Found a Hidden Swarm of Space Junk Threatening Earth's Most Valuable Orbit  Gizmodo

  4. Adapting Satellite Design to the Growing Space Debris Problem  AZoQuantum
  5. Tiny 2-inch space debris discovered in key satellite orbit, scientists warn  The News International

item.title: Bacteria Are Thriving In a Radioactive Former Soviet Mine – Leaving Only 5% of The Uranium Behind - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Bacteria Are Thriving In a Radioactive Former Soviet Mine – Leaving Only 5% of The Uranium Behind  ScienceAlert
  2. Bacteria turn dissolved uranium into stable compound in 130 days, study finds  Phys.org
  3. Bacteria Turn Toxic Uranium Into a Surprisingly Stable Compound  SciTechDaily
  4. These Microbes Removed 96% of Dissolved Uranium From Mine Water  ZME Science
  5. Bacteria may offer breakthrough solution for uranium pollution, researchers say  Mid-day

item.title: Researchers froze the simplest amino acid to nearly minus 260°C and bombarded it with radiation that mimics cosmic rays — and it formed peptides, the building blocks of proteins, under conditions that exist before stars and planets are born - Space Daily
item.description: Researchers froze the simplest amino acid to nearly minus 260°C and bombarded it with radiation that mimics cosmic rays — and it formed peptides, the building blocks of proteins, under conditions that exist before stars and planets are born  Space Daily

item.title: Spacecraft Heading for Asteroid NASA Slapped so Hard It Changed Its Orbit Gets Crucial OTA Update - autoevolution
item.description:
1. Spacecraft Heading for Asteroid NASA Slapped so Hard It Changed Its Orbit Gets Crucial OTA Update  autoevolution
  2. A spacecraft called DART was deliberately crashed into an asteroid in 2022 to test whether humanity could deflect one, and it worked — the asteroid's orbit changed measurably, the first time humans have intentionally altered the movement of a natural body in  ScienceBlog.com
  3. NASA mission changed orbit of asteroid around Sun  The Grainger College of Engineering
  4. What Hera Will Find At The Asteroid DART Crashed Into  WorldAtlas

item.description:

  3. Climatic reach of small-scale turbulence in the ocean interior  Nature
  4. Hidden deep-sea turbulence could alter climate and fisheries within one lifetime  Phys.org

item.title: Birds are not descended from dinosaurs — they are dinosaurs, a living branch of the theropod family that survived the asteroid and now fills the world with more than 10,000 species - Space Daily
item.description:

  3. From T. rex to turkeys: Steve Brusatte on ‘The Story of Birds’  WGN Radio 720

item.title: The most common type of planet discovered in the Milky Way may be a world that does not exist in our solar system — larger than Earth, smaller than Neptune, and wrapped in an atmosphere we barely understand - Space Daily
item.description: The most common type of planet discovered in the Milky Way may be a world that does not exist in our solar system — larger than Earth, smaller than Neptune, and wrapped in an atmosphere we barely understand  Space Daily

item.title: Light from the Sun takes about eight minutes to reach Earth, but the energy inside a single photon can spend tens of thousands of years bouncing through the Sun's dense core before it ever escapes the surface to begin that eight-minute trip - Space Daily
item.description: Light from the Sun takes about eight minutes to reach Earth, but the energy inside a single photon can spend tens of thousands of years bouncing through the Sun's dense core before it ever escapes the surface to begin that eight-minute trip  Space Daily

item.description: Atoms tell different stories when light hits a molecule in trillionths of a second  Phys.org

item.title: A rare meteorite examined in 2026 revealed evidence of a Moon-sized world that orbited the young Sun more than 4.5 billion years ago, then vanished — possibly shattered in a catastrophic collision while Earth was still being assembled - Space Daily
item.description: A rare meteorite examined in 2026 revealed evidence of a Moon-sized world that orbited the young Sun more than 4.5 billion years ago, then vanished — possibly shattered in a catastrophic collision while Earth was still being assembled  Space Daily

item.title: A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about four billion tonnes on Earth — roughly the mass of a mountain — because the star has crushed the equivalent of the Sun into a sphere the width of a city - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about four billion tonnes on Earth — roughly the mass of a mountain — because the star has crushed the equivalent of the Sun into a sphere the width of a city  Space Daily
  2. A neutron star can pack more mass than our entire Sun into a sphere only about 20 kilometres across — roughly the size of a city — and a teaspoon of its material would weigh about a billion tonnes on Earth, more than every human being alive combined.  Space Daily

item.title: Astronomers have found a super-Earth just 25 light-years away — close enough by cosmic standards that it sits in the small category of worlds scientists consider genuinely worth searching for signs of life - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Astronomers have found a super-Earth just 25 light-years away — close enough by cosmic standards that it sits in the small category of worlds scientists consider genuinely worth searching for signs of life  Space Daily
  2. Scientists Say They’ve Identified an Earth-Like Planet Right Next Door  Futurism
  3. Alien life on nearby exoplanet more likely than previously thought  The Jerusalem Post
  4. Astronomers have spent decades searching nearby stars for another Earth, and now they may have found one just 25 light-years away  The Times of India
  5. Mass revision boosts habitability odds for nearby super-Earth  MSN
